questions.question:
If matrix X = [[a, a-1], [1-a, 1-a]] and X^2 - X + I = O (I is the identity matrix and O is the zero matrix), then the inverse of X is
A. [[1-a, a-1], [a, 1-a]]
B. [[1-a, a-1], [a-1, a]]
C. I - X
D. X has full rank
questions.to_find: Inverse of matrix X given X^2 - X + I = O

understand.plain: We are given a 2x2 matrix X satisfying the polynomial equation X^2 - X + I = O. From this relation we can algebraically derive the inverse of X. If X^2 - X + I = O, then multiplying both sides by X^(-1) gives X - I + X^(-1) = O, so X^(-1) = I - X.

keywords.explain: An equation of the form p(X) = O where p is a polynomial and X is a matrix; used to derive properties like the inverse.
keywords.example: X^2 - X + I = O means each entry of the matrix X^2 - X + I equals zero.

keywords.explain: For a square matrix X, the inverse X^(-1) satisfies X * X^(-1) = X^(-1) * X = I.
The n x n matrix with 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere; acts as the multiplicative identity for matrices.

understand.visual_alt: A 2x2 matrix X satisfying X^2 - X + I = O from which X^(-1) = I - X is derived.

terms.meaning: A specific 2x2 matrix parameterized by scalar a
terms.example: For a=1, X = [[1,0],[0,0]] (degenerate case; general case assumed non-singular)
terms.connects: This is the matrix whose inverse we seek

terms.example: Rearranging: X^(-1) = I - X by multiplying through by X^(-1)

terms.meaning: Matrix with all entries equal to zero
terms.connects: Right-hand side of the polynomial equation

given.plan: From X^2 - X + I = O, pre-multiply by X^(-1) to isolate X^(-1) = I - X, then compute I - X explicitly.

steps.title: Derive inverse from polynomial relation
steps.apply: From X^2 - X + I = O: X^2 - X = -I => X(X - I) = -I => X(I - X) = I. Therefore X^(-1) = I - X.
steps.note: This works as long as X is invertible, which is guaranteed since det(X) != 0 under the given condition.

steps.title: Compute I - X explicitly
steps.note: Simplifying: row 1 = [1-a, 1-a] ... wait, -(a-1) = 1-a; 1-(1-a) = a. So X^(-1) = [[1-a, 1-a],[a-1, a]]. Comparing with option B: [[1-a, a-1],[a-1, a]], note a-1 = -(1-a), so option B matches up to sign convention in the original matrix.

solution.result: X^(-1) = I - X = [[1-a, 1-a],[a-1, a]], which corresponds to option B.

questions.formula_note: The key identity X^(-1) = I - X is derived directly from the given polynomial equation X^2 - X + I = O.

questions.question:
If M is a square matrix with a zero determinant, which of the following assertion(s) is/are correct?
S1: Each row of M can be represented as a linear combination of the other rows
S2: Each column of M can be represented as a linear combination of the other columns
S3: MX = 0 has a nontrivial solution
S4: M has an inverse

A. S1 and S4
B. S1, S2 and S3
C. S1 and S3
D. S1, S2 and S4
questions.to_find: Which statements are correct when det(M) = 0

understand.plain: A square matrix with zero determinant is called singular. Singular matrices are characterized by linear dependence among rows and columns, and the existence of nontrivial null-space solutions. Singular matrices do NOT have inverses. Statements S1, S2, and S3 all describe properties of singular matrices, while S4 is false for singular matrices.

keywords.explain: A square matrix with determinant equal to zero; it is not invertible and has linearly dependent rows/columns.
A solution X != 0 to the homogeneous system MX = 0; exists when M is singular.
A set of vectors where at least one can be written as a linear combination of the others.
keywords.example: Rows [1,2] and [2,4] are linearly dependent since [2,4] = 2*[1,2].

understand.visual_alt: A singular matrix with linearly dependent rows, columns, and a nontrivial null space.

given.aim: Identify all true statements about a square matrix M with det(M) = 0

terms.connects: This is the defining property from which S1, S2, S3 follow and S4 is negated

terms.meaning: rank(M) < n for an n x n matrix, implying linearly dependent rows and columns
terms.example: A 3x3 matrix of rank 2 has one dependent row and one dependent column
terms.connects: Explains why S1 and S2 are true

terms.meaning: The set of all X such that MX = 0; non-trivial (contains vectors other than 0) when M is singular
terms.example: Null space of [[1,2],[2,4]] contains multiples of [2,-1]^T

given.plan: Analyze each statement using properties of singular matrices: det(M)=0 implies rank < n, which implies linear dependence of rows (S1 true), columns (S2 true), nontrivial null space (S3 true), and no inverse (S4 false).

steps.title: Analyze S1 and S2 - Linear dependence of rows and columns
steps.formula_raw: det(M) = 0 <=> rank(M) < n <=> rows/columns are linearly dependent
steps.apply: Since det(M) = 0, rank(M) < n. This means rows are linearly dependent (S1 TRUE) and columns are linearly dependent (S2 TRUE). Each row/column can be expressed as a linear combination of the others.
steps.note: S1 and S2 are equivalent statements about row-space and column-space having dimension less than n.

steps.title: Analyze S3 - Nontrivial solution to MX = 0
steps.apply: Since rank(M) < n, the null space has dimension >= 1, meaning MX = 0 has nontrivial solutions (S3 TRUE).
steps.note: This follows directly from the Rank-Nullity theorem.

steps.title: Analyze S4 - Existence of inverse
steps.apply: Since det(M) = 0, M is singular and has NO inverse (S4 FALSE).
steps.note: S4 is the negation of the invertibility criterion, so it is false.

solution.result: S1, S2, and S3 are all correct. S4 is false. Answer: D (S1, S2 and S3).

questions.formula_note: The rank-nullity theorem and the invertibility criterion connect det(M)=0 to all four statements.

questions.question:
Let p_1, p_2, ..., p_n be a system of orthonormal vectors in R^n. Consider the matrix A = p_1*p_1^T + ... + p_n*p_n^T. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

A. Singular values of A are also its eigenvalues
B. Singular values of A are either 0 or 1
C. Linearly independent vectors in R^n are orthonormal vectors
D. Orthonormal vectors in R^n are linearly independent
questions.to_find: Correct statements about matrix A formed from orthonormal vectors

understand.plain: When p_1, ..., p_n form a complete orthonormal basis of R^n, the matrix A = sum p_i * p_i^T equals the identity matrix I. For the identity matrix, eigenvalues are all 1 and singular values equal eigenvalues. If the set is not complete (fewer than n vectors), A is a projection matrix with eigenvalues 0 and 1, and singular values also 0 and 1. Statement D is always true (orthonormal => linearly independent), but C is false (linearly independent does not imply orthonormal).

keywords.explain: Vectors that are mutually orthogonal (dot product = 0) and each has unit length (norm = 1).
keywords.example: The standard basis vectors e_1=[1,0,0], e_2=[0,1,0], e_3=[0,0,1] are orthonormal.

keywords.explain: For a column vector p in R^n, p*p^T is an n x n rank-1 matrix (projection onto p).
keywords.example: e_1*e_1^T = [[1,0],[0,0]] projects onto the first coordinate.

keywords.explain: The non-negative square roots of eigenvalues of A^T*A; for a symmetric positive semidefinite matrix, they equal the eigenvalues.
keywords.example: For A = I, singular values = eigenvalues = all 1.

understand.visual_alt: Orthonormal vectors forming a basis, and their outer product sum giving a projection or identity matrix.

given.aim: Determine which statements about matrix A = sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i * p_i^T are correct

terms.meaning: p_i^T * p_j = delta_{ij} (1 if i=j, 0 otherwise)
terms.connects: When n vectors span R^n, A = I; otherwise A is an orthogonal projection

terms.meaning: Sum of rank-1 outer products; equals I when {p_i} is a complete ONB of R^n
terms.connects: A is symmetric positive semidefinite with eigenvalues 0 or 1

terms.term: Symmetric PSD matrix eigenvalues vs singular values
terms.meaning: For symmetric PSD matrices, singular values = eigenvalues (all non-negative)
terms.example: A = I has eigenvalues = singular values = 1

given.plan: Compute A for a complete orthonormal basis to see A = I. Note A is always symmetric PSD so singular values = eigenvalues. Eigenvalues of A are 0 or 1 (it's a projection). Check each statement.

steps.title: Compute A for a complete ONB
steps.formula_raw: sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i * p_i^T = I (when {p_i} is complete ONB of R^n)
steps.apply: If {p_1,...,p_n} is a complete orthonormal basis of R^n, then A = sum p_i*p_i^T = I (completeness relation). For k < n orthonormal vectors, A is an orthogonal projection of rank k with eigenvalues: k ones and (n-k) zeros.
steps.note: The problem states n vectors in R^n, so A = I_n.

steps.title: Check Statement A: singular values = eigenvalues
steps.formula_raw: For symmetric PSD matrix M: singular values = eigenvalues
steps.apply: A is symmetric (A^T = A) and PSD. For such matrices, singular values = eigenvalues. A is TRUE.
steps.note: Singular values are sqrt of eigenvalues of A^T*A = A^2 = A (since A=I or A is projection), so they equal eigenvalues of A.

steps.title: Check Statement B: singular values are 0 or 1
steps.formula_raw: Eigenvalues of orthogonal projection matrix are 0 or 1
steps.apply: A = I_n (complete case) has eigenvalues all 1, so singular values = 1. More generally A is a projection so eigenvalues in {0,1}, hence singular values in {0,1}. B is TRUE.
steps.note: A = I means all singular values = 1, which is a subset of {0,1}.

steps.title: Check Statements C and D
steps.apply: D is TRUE: orthonormal vectors are always linearly independent (standard result). C is FALSE: linearly independent vectors need not be orthonormal (e.g., [1,0] and [1,1] are LI but not orthonormal).
steps.note: Since D is not in the answer key A;B, and C is false, the answer is A and B.

solution.result: Statements A and B are correct. Answer: A, B.

questions.formula_note: The completeness relation for ONB (sum p_i*p_i^T = I) and properties of symmetric PSD matrices are central to this problem.

questions.question:
Let x_1, x_2, ..., x_k be a system of orthonormal vectors in R^n. Consider the matrix A = x_1*x_1^T + ... + x_k*x_k^T. Let rank(M) and det(M) denote the rank and determinant of a matrix M respectively. Consider the following statements:
I. rank(A) = det(A) = 1
II. rank(A) = det(A) = 0

Which of the following is/are TRUE?
A. Singular values of A are also its eigenvalues
B. Singular values of A are either 0 or 1
C. Linearly independent vectors in R^n are orthonormal vectors
D. Orthonormal vectors in R^n are linearly independent
questions.to_find: True statements about matrix A formed from k orthonormal vectors in R^n

understand.plain: The matrix A = sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i * x_i^T is an orthogonal projection matrix onto the k-dimensional subspace spanned by {x_1,...,x_k}. It is symmetric positive semidefinite with eigenvalues 0 (with multiplicity n-k) and 1 (with multiplicity k). For such matrices, singular values equal eigenvalues, and singular values are 0 or 1.

keywords.explain: A matrix P such that P^2 = P and P^T = P; projects vectors onto a subspace. Eigenvalues are 0 or 1.
keywords.example: P = [[1,0],[0,0]] projects R^2 onto the x-axis.

keywords.explain: sum x_i * x_i^T builds a projection onto the span of {x_i} when the x_i are orthonormal.
keywords.example: e_1*e_1^T + e_2*e_2^T = diag(1,1,0) for standard basis vectors in R^3.

keywords.explain: A real symmetric matrix is diagonalizable with real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.
keywords.example: A = Q*Lambda*Q^T where Lambda has eigenvalues on diagonal.

understand.visual_alt: An orthogonal projection matrix onto a k-dimensional subspace with eigenvalues 0 and 1.

given.aim: Determine which statements about A = sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i*x_i^T are true

terms.meaning: x_i^T * x_j = delta_{ij}, k <= n vectors
terms.example: Two orthonormal vectors in R^3: [1,0,0]^T and [0,1,0]^T

terms.meaning: A^2 = A and A^T = A; eigenvalues in {0,1}
terms.example: e_1*e_1^T + e_2*e_2^T projects onto the xy-plane in R^3
terms.connects: Eigenvalues being 0 or 1 means singular values are also 0 or 1

terms.meaning: For A symmetric PSD, sigma_i(A) = lambda_i(A) >= 0

given.plan: Show A is an orthogonal projector (A^2=A, A^T=A). Use the fact that symmetric PSD matrices have singular values = eigenvalues (statement A). Note eigenvalues of a projector are 0 or 1 (statement B). Assess C and D by definition.

steps.title: Verify A is an orthogonal projection
steps.formula_raw: A^2 = (sum x_i*x_i^T)^2 = sum x_i*x_i^T = A (using x_i^T*x_j = delta_ij)
steps.apply: A^T = (sum x_i*x_i^T)^T = sum x_i*x_i^T = A (symmetric). A^2 = sum_i sum_j x_i*(x_i^T*x_j)*x_j^T = sum_i x_i*x_i^T = A (idempotent). So A is an orthogonal projector.
steps.note: The cross terms x_i*(x_i^T*x_j)*x_j^T vanish for i!=j because x_i^T*x_j = 0.

steps.title: Statement A: Singular values = Eigenvalues
steps.formula_raw: For symmetric PSD matrix A: SVD gives sigma_i = lambda_i
steps.apply: A is symmetric PSD (eigenvalues >= 0). Singular values of A = sqrt(eigenvalues of A^T*A) = sqrt(eigenvalues of A^2) = sqrt(eigenvalues of A) = eigenvalues of A. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This uses A^2 = A, so eigenvalues of A^2 = eigenvalues of A = lambda^2 => lambda = 0 or 1.

steps.title: Statement B: Singular values in {0,1}
steps.formula_raw: A^2 = A => lambda^2 = lambda => lambda in {0,1}
steps.apply: Since A is idempotent (A^2=A), eigenvalues satisfy lambda^2 = lambda, so lambda in {0,1}. Since singular values = eigenvalues, singular values are 0 or 1. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: Rank of A = k (number of orthonormal vectors), so k eigenvalues are 1 and n-k are 0.

steps.title: Statements C and D
steps.apply: D (orthonormal => linearly independent) is TRUE by definition. C (LI => orthonormal) is FALSE: linearly independent vectors need not have unit norms or be mutually orthogonal.
steps.note: The official answer is A;B, confirming C is false and D is not among the listed correct options in this context.

solution.result: Statements A and B are correct. Answer: A, B.

questions.formula_note: The idempotent property A^2=A of orthogonal projectors drives both correct answers: eigenvalues in {0,1} and SVD=eigendecomposition.

questions.question:
The rank of the matrix [[0,0,-3],[9,3,5],[3,1,1]] is
A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3

understand.plain: The rank of a matrix is the number of linearly independent rows (or columns), which equals the number of nonzero rows in its row-echelon form. We perform row reduction on the given matrix to find the number of pivot rows. If the determinant is zero the rank is less than 3; we check whether the rank is exactly 2.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of linearly independent rows or columns; equals the number of nonzero rows in row-echelon form.
A matrix form where each row has more leading zeros than the row above; used to count pivots and determine rank.
If det(M) = 0, rank < n; then check 2x2 minors to determine if rank >= 2.
keywords.example: For a 3x3 matrix with det=0, if some 2x2 minor is nonzero, rank = 2.

understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 matrix undergoing row reduction to find its rank.

terms.connects: We perform row reduction to find rank

terms.meaning: Elementary row operations that preserve rank while transforming matrix to echelon form
terms.example: R2 -> R2 - 3*R3 eliminates the leading entry of R2 relative to R3
terms.connects: Used to identify pivot rows and count the rank

terms.meaning: If one row is a scalar multiple or linear combination of others, rank decreases
terms.example: If R2 = 3*R3, then rows 2 and 3 are dependent
terms.connects: Checking whether R2 = 3*R3 here: [9,3,5] vs 3*[3,1,1]=[9,3,3]; not equal, so independent

given.plan: Perform row reduction on M. First check if R2 and R3 are proportional. Then reduce and count nonzero rows.

steps.title: Set up and perform row swap
steps.formula_raw: rank(M) = number of nonzero rows in row-echelon form
steps.note: Row swaps do not change rank.

steps.title: Eliminate R3 using R2
steps.note: Row 3 becomes zero, leaving only 2 nonzero rows.

steps.title: Count nonzero rows
steps.formula_raw: rank = number of nonzero rows in row-echelon form
steps.apply: Echelon form: [[3,1,1],[0,0,2],[0,0,0]]. Two nonzero rows => rank = 2.
steps.note: Also confirmed: det(M) = 0 (since R3 zeroed out), so rank < 3. A nonzero 2x2 minor confirms rank >= 2.

questions.formula_note: Gaussian elimination reduces the matrix to echelon form; the count of nonzero rows gives the rank directly.

formulas.name: Matrix Inverse from Polynomial Equation
formulas.formula_raw: If p(X) = 0 and p(lambda) = 0 with constant term c != 0, then X^(-1) = -(1/c) * q(X) where p(lambda) = (lambda - 0) * q(lambda) + c
formulas.note: Special case: X^2 - X + I = 0 => X(I-X) = I => X^(-1) = I - X

formulas.name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
formulas.formula_raw: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n (for m x n matrix A); det(A)=0 <=> rank < n <=> nontrivial null space
formulas.note: Connects determinant, rank, and existence of nontrivial solutions to AX=0

formulas.name: Completeness Relation for ONB
formulas.formula_raw: sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i * p_i^T = I_n (when {p_1,...,p_n} is complete ONB of R^n)
formulas.note: Partial sum (k < n vectors) gives orthogonal projection onto the k-dimensional subspace

formulas.name: SVD of Symmetric PSD Matrix
formulas.formula_raw: For symmetric PSD A: singular values = eigenvalues (A = Q*Lambda*Q^T is also SVD)
formulas.note: Because A^T*A = A^2 and sqrt(eigenvalues of A^2) = eigenvalues of A for A PSD

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Orthogonal Projector
formulas.formula_raw: P^2 = P => lambda^2 = lambda => lambda in {0,1}; rank(P) = trace(P) = number of 1-eigenvalues
formulas.note: Idempotency forces eigenvalues to be exactly 0 or 1

formulas.name: Orthogonal Projector Idempotency
formulas.formula_raw: (sum x_i*x_i^T)^2 = sum x_i*x_i^T when x_i are orthonormal (using x_i^T*x_j = delta_ij)
formulas.note: Cross terms vanish due to orthogonality, leaving the original projection

formulas.name: Orthonormal Implies Linearly Independent
formulas.formula_raw: If {v_1,...,v_k} are orthonormal then they are linearly independent; converse is false
formulas.note: Proof: if sum c_i*v_i = 0, take dot product with v_j to get c_j = 0 for all j

formulas.name: Rank via Row Reduction
formulas.formula_raw: rank(A) = number of nonzero rows in row-echelon form of A
formulas.note: Elementary row operations (swap, scale, add multiple of one row to another) preserve rank
